title: König Drosselbart
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 52. Text from Wikisource („König Drosselbart (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The fifty-second tale (KHM 52). A haughty princess mocks every suitor, and her father marries her in anger to a beggar-musician; humbled through poverty and menial work, she learns her husband was the very king she scorned. Four short rhymes punctuate the tale; 1857 spelling is preserved (roth, Hochmuth, gieng, Vorrath).

sentences.t: Ein König hatte eine Tochter, die war über alle Maßen schön, aber dabei so stolz und übermüthig, daß ihr kein Freier gut genug war.
sentences.l: A king had a daughter, who was beautiful beyond all measure, but withal so proud and haughty, that no suitor was good enough for her.
sentences.i: A king had a daughter who was beautiful beyond all measure, but so proud and haughty besides that no suitor was good enough for her.
sentences.n: “über alle Maßen” = beyond all measure (Maßen is an old plural of Maß). dabei here = “along with it, withal.” daß (1857 spelling of dass) marks the ‘that’-clause and sends its verb to the end.

sentences.t: Sie wies einen nach dem andern ab, und trieb noch dazu Spott mit ihnen.
sentences.l: She turned one after the other away, and on top of that carried on mockery with them.
sentences.i: She turned one suitor after another away, and made mock of them into the bargain.
sentences.n: abweisen (to turn away, reject) is separable: “wies… ab.” “Spott treiben mit” = to make mockery of, poke fun at — you will meet Spott and spotten again and again in this tale.

sentences.t: Einmal ließ der König ein großes Fest anstellen, und ladete dazu aus der Nähe und Ferne die heirathslustigen Männer ein.
sentences.l: Once the king had a great feast arranged, and invited to it from near and far the marriage-minded men.
sentences.i: Once the king had a great feast held, and invited to it the marriageable men from near and far.
sentences.n: einladen (to invite) is separable: “ladete… ein”; ladete is an older weak past of laden. heirathslustig (1857 spelling) = eager to marry (Heirath ‘marriage’ + lustig).

sentences.t: Sie wurden alle in eine Reihe nach Rang und Stand geordnet; erst kamen die Könige, dann die Herzöge, die Fürsten, Grafen und Freiherrn, zuletzt die Edelleute.
sentences.l: They were all ranged in a row according to rank and station; first came the kings, then the dukes, the princes, counts and barons, last the noblemen.
sentences.i: They were all set out in a row by rank and station: first came the kings, then the dukes, the princes, counts and barons, and last the noblemen.
sentences.n: “nach Rang und Stand” = by rank and station. A descending ladder of nobility — kings down to plain noblemen.

sentences.t: Nun ward die Königstochter durch die Reihen geführt, aber an jedem hatte sie etwas auszusetzen.
sentences.l: Now the king’s daughter was led through the rows, but on each one she had something to find fault with.
sentences.i: Now the king’s daughter was led along the rows, but with every one of them she found something to criticize.
sentences.n: ward is the archaic simple past of werden (became/was) — everywhere in Grimm. “etwas auszusetzen haben (an)” = to have something to criticize (about); auszusetzen recurs below.

sentences.t: Der eine war ihr zu dick, „das Weinfaß!“ sprach sie.
sentences.l: The one was for her too fat, “the wine-cask!” said she.
sentences.i: The first was too fat for her: “the wine-barrel!” she said.

sentences.t: Der andere zu lang, „lang und schwank hat keinen Gang.“
sentences.l: The other too tall, “tall and lanky has no proper gait.”
sentences.i: The next too tall: “tall and lanky has no proper gait.”
sentences.n: One of her mocking rhymes: “lang und schwank hat keinen Gang” — schwank = slender, swaying; Gang = gait, bearing. It rhymes lang/Gang.

sentences.t: Der dritte zu kurz, „kurz und dick hat kein Geschick.“
sentences.l: The third too short, “short and stout has no knack.”
sentences.i: The third too short: “short and stout has no knack.”
sentences.n: Rhyme again: “kurz und dick hat kein Geschick” (dick/Geschick). Geschick = skill, deftness — a short fat man is called clumsy.

sentences.t: Der vierte zu blaß, „der bleiche Tod!“ der fünfte zu roth, „der Zinshahn!“ der sechste war nicht gerad genug, „grünes Holz, hinterm Ofen getrocknet!“
sentences.l: The fourth too pale, “pale Death!” the fifth too red, “the rent-cock!” the sixth was not straight enough, “green wood, dried behind the stove!”
sentences.i: The fourth too pale — “Death himself!”; the fifth too red — “the red rooster!”; the sixth not straight enough — “green wood, dried behind the stove!”
sentences.n: A run of jibes. “der bleiche Tod” = pale Death. der Zinshahn = the ‘rent-cock,’ a red cockerel once handed over as rent — a gibe at a red-faced man. gerad (1857 for gerade) = straight; a crooked man is “green wood, warped from drying behind the stove.”

sentences.t: Und so hatte sie an einem jeden etwas auszusetzen, besonders aber machte sie sich über einen guten König lustig, der ganz oben stand, und dem das Kinn ein wenig krumm gewachsen war.
sentences.l: And so she had on every single one something to find fault with, but especially she made fun of one good king, who stood quite at the top, and whose chin had grown a little crooked.
sentences.i: And so she found fault with every single one of them; but above all she made fun of one good king who stood right at the top, and whose chin had grown a little crooked.
sentences.n: “sich über… lustig machen” = to make fun of, poke fun at. “dem das Kinn… gewachsen war” — the dative dem is possessive: “whose chin had grown crooked.”

sentences.t: „Ei,“ rief sie und lachte, „der hat ein Kinn, wie die Drossel einen Schnabel;“ und seit der Zeit bekam er den Namen Drosselbart.
sentences.l: “Ah,” cried she and laughed, “that one has a chin like the thrush has a beak;” and from that time on he got the name Thrushbeard.
sentences.i: “Why,” she cried, laughing, “that one has a chin like a thrush’s beak!” — and from that time on he was called Thrushbeard.
sentences.n: The tale’s title moment. die Drossel = the thrush; a Drossel has no ‘beard,’ so the name mocks the little tuft under his crooked chin. Drosselbart = Drossel (thrush) + Bart (beard).

sentences.t: Der alte König aber, als er sah daß seine Tochter nichts that als über die Leute spotten, und alle Freier, die da versammelt waren, verschmähte, ward er zornig und schwur, sie sollte den ersten besten Bettler zum Manne nehmen, der vor seine Thüre käme.
sentences.l: But the old king, when he saw that his daughter did nothing but mock at people, and spurned all the suitors who were gathered there, he grew wrathful and swore she should take the very first beggar for a husband who came before his door.
sentences.i: But the old king, when he saw that his daughter did nothing but mock at people and spurned every suitor gathered there, grew angry and swore that she should take the very first beggar to come to his door as her husband.
sentences.n: “nichts that als… spotten” = did nothing but mock (that is 1857 spelling of tat, past of thun/tun). “der erste beste” = the very first that comes along, any old one. schwur is an archaic past of schwören (swore). käme is subjunctive (whoever might come).

sentences.t: Ein paar Tage darauf hub ein Spielmann an unter dem Fenster zu singen, um damit ein geringes Almosen zu verdienen.
sentences.l: A couple of days afterward a minstrel struck up singing under the window, in order to earn thereby a small alms.
sentences.i: A couple of days later a minstrel struck up a song beneath the window, to earn some small alms by it.
sentences.n: hub… an = began (anheben, separable; hub is an archaic past of heben, ‘to raise/lift up’). ein Spielmann = a wandering minstrel. “um… zu” + infinitive = in order to.

sentences.t: Als es der König hörte, sprach er „laßt ihn herauf kommen.“
sentences.l: When the king heard it, he spoke “let him come up.”
sentences.i: When the king heard it, he said, “Let him come up.”

sentences.t: Da trat der Spielmann in seinen schmutzigen verlumpten Kleidern herein, sang vor dem König und seiner Tochter, und bat, als er fertig war, um eine milde Gabe.
sentences.l: Then stepped the minstrel in his dirty tattered clothes in, sang before the king and his daughter, and begged, when he was finished, for a charitable gift.
sentences.i: Then the minstrel stepped in, in his dirty, tattered clothes, sang before the king and his daughter, and, when he had finished, begged for alms.
sentences.n: hereintreten (to step in) is separable: “trat… herein.” verlumpt = ragged, in tatters. “milde Gabe” = alms, a charitable gift.

sentences.t: Der König sprach „dein Gesang hat mir so wohl gefallen, daß ich dir meine Tochter da zur Frau geben will.“
sentences.l: The king spoke “your singing has pleased me so well, that I will give you my daughter here to wife.”
sentences.i: The king said, “Your singing has pleased me so well that I will give you my daughter here to wife.”
sentences.n: “zur Frau geben” = to give in marriage (literally ‘give to wife’). gefallen = to please: “dein Gesang hat mir gefallen” = ‘your song has pleased me.’

sentences.t: Die Königstochter erschrack, aber der König sagte „ich habe den Eid gethan, dich dem ersten besten Bettelmann zu geben, den will ich auch halten.“
sentences.l: The king’s daughter took fright, but the king said “I have taken the oath to give you to the very first beggar-man; that I will indeed keep.”
sentences.i: The king’s daughter was horrified, but the king said, “I have sworn the oath to give you to the very first beggar-man, and keep it I will.”
sentences.n: erschrack = took fright (archaic past of erschrecken). “den Eid thun/gethan” = to take/swear the oath (gethan is 1857 for getan). den will ich… halten — the fronted den (‘that one, i.e. the oath’) points back for emphasis.

sentences.t: Es half keine Einrede, der Pfarrer ward geholt, und sie mußte sich gleich mit dem Spielmann trauen lassen.
sentences.l: No objection availed, the parson was fetched, and she had to let herself be wedded to the minstrel at once.
sentences.i: No protest availed; the parson was fetched, and she had to let herself be married to the minstrel then and there.
sentences.n: “es half keine Einrede” = no objection helped (i.e. was of any use). “sich trauen lassen” = to have oneself married (trauen = to wed a couple). mußte = had to.

sentences.t: Als das geschehen war, sprach der König, „nun schickt sichs nicht, daß du als ein Bettelweib noch länger in meinem Schloß bleibst, du kannst nur mit deinem Manne fortziehen.“
sentences.l: When that had happened, the king spoke, “now it is not fitting that you as a beggar-woman remain any longer in my castle; you can only move off with your husband.”
sentences.i: When that was done, the king said, “Now it is not seemly that you should stay any longer in my castle as a beggar-woman; you can only go off with your husband.”
sentences.n: “sich schicken” = to be fitting/proper; “nun schickt sichs nicht” = now it is not seemly (sichs = sich es). fortziehen (to move away) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Bettelmann führte sie an der Hand hinaus, und sie mußte mit ihm zu Fuß fort gehen.
sentences.l: The beggar-man led her out by the hand, and she had to go off with him on foot.
sentences.i: The beggar-man led her out by the hand, and she had to set off with him on foot.
sentences.n: “an der Hand” = by the hand. “zu Fuß” = on foot. fortgehen (to go off) is separable: “fort gehen.”

sentences.t: Als sie in einen großen Wald kamen, da fragte sie
sentences.l: When they came into a great forest, then she asked
sentences.i: When they came into a great forest, she asked:

sentences.t:
„ach, wem gehört der schöne Wald?“
„Der gehört dem König Drosselbart;
hättst du’n genommen, so wär er dein.“
„Ich arme Jungfer zart,
ach, hätt ich genommen den König Drosselbart!“
sentences.l:
“ah, to whom belongs the fair forest?”
“It belongs to King Thrushbeard;
had you taken him, then it would be yours.”
“I poor tender maiden,
ah, had I but taken King Thrushbeard!”
sentences.i:
“Ah, to whom does the fair forest belong?”
“It belongs to King Thrushbeard;
had you taken him, it would be yours.”
“Poor tender maid that I am —
ah, had I but taken King Thrushbeard!”
sentences.n: The first of three matching refrains as they cross his lands. “hättst du’n genommen” = “had you taken him” (hättst = hättest; du’n = du ihn, ‘n a colloquial contraction of ihn). “so wär er dein” = then it would be yours (dein = yours, addressing her) — wär is subjunctive (wäre). “Jungfer zart” = tender maiden (poetic word order, adjective after noun for the rhyme with Drosselbart).

sentences.t: Darauf kamen sie über eine Wiese, da fragte sie wieder
sentences.l: Thereupon they came over a meadow, there she asked again
sentences.i: Then they came across a meadow, and again she asked:

sentences.t:
„wem gehört die schöne grüne Wiese?“
„Sie gehört dem König Drosselbart;
hättst du’n genommen, so wär sie dein.“
„Ich arme Jungfer zart,
ach, hätt ich genommen den König Drosselbart!“
sentences.l:
“to whom belongs the fair green meadow?”
“It belongs to King Thrushbeard;
had you taken him, then it would be yours.”
“I poor tender maiden,
ah, had I but taken King Thrushbeard!”
sentences.i:
“To whom does the fair green meadow belong?”
“It belongs to King Thrushbeard;
had you taken him, it would be yours.”
“Poor tender maid that I am —
ah, had I but taken King Thrushbeard!”
sentences.n: The refrain repeats, only the possession changing (forest → meadow → city → hovel). The threefold repetition, with a twist at the end, is the tale’s backbone.

sentences.t: Dann kamen sie durch eine große Stadt, da fragte sie wieder
sentences.l: Then they came through a great city, there she asked again
sentences.i: Then they came through a great city, and again she asked:

sentences.t:
„wem gehört diese schöne große Stadt?“
„Sie gehört dem König Drosselbart;
hättst du’n genommen, so wär sie dein.“
„Ich arme Jungfer zart,
ach, hätt ich genommen den König Drosselbart!“
sentences.l:
“to whom belongs this fair great city?”
“It belongs to King Thrushbeard;
had you taken him, then it would be yours.”
“I poor tender maiden,
ah, had I but taken King Thrushbeard!”
sentences.i:
“To whom does this fair great city belong?”
“It belongs to King Thrushbeard;
had you taken him, it would be yours.”
“Poor tender maid that I am —
ah, had I but taken King Thrushbeard!”

sentences.t: „Es gefällt mir gar nicht,“ sprach der Spielmann, „daß du dir immer einen andern zum Mann wünschest: bin ich dir nicht gut genug?“
sentences.l: “It does not please me at all,” spoke the minstrel, “that you always wish yourself another for a husband: am I not good enough for you?”
sentences.i: “It doesn’t please me at all,” said the minstrel, “that you keep wishing for another man: am I not good enough for you?”
sentences.n: “es gefällt mir gar nicht” = it doesn’t please me at all. wünschest is the older 2nd-singular ending (modern wünschst). “dir… einen andern wünschen” = to wish for oneself another.

sentences.t: Endlich kamen sie an ein ganz kleines Häuschen, da sprach sie
sentences.l: At last they came to a quite small little house, there she spoke
sentences.i: At last they came to a tiny little house, and she said:

sentences.t:
„ach, Gott, was ist das Haus so klein!
wem mag das elende winzige Häuschen sein?“
sentences.l:
“ah, God, what is the house so small!
to whom may the wretched tiny little-house be?”
sentences.i: “Ah, God, how small the house is! To whom can the wretched, tiny little house belong?”
sentences.n: The fourth ‘asking’ breaks the pattern — this time the humble house is her own. “wem mag… sein” = to whom can it belong (mögen expressing possibility). Häuschen and winzig underline how far she has fallen.

sentences.t: Der Spielmann antwortete „das ist mein und dein Haus, wo wir zusammen wohnen.“
sentences.l: The minstrel answered “that is my and your house, where we dwell together.”
sentences.i: The minstrel answered, “That is your house and mine, where we live together.”

sentences.t: Sie mußte sich bücken, damit sie zu der niedrigen Thür hinein kam.
sentences.l: She had to stoop down, so that she got in through the low door.
sentences.i: She had to stoop to get in through the low door.
sentences.n: “sich bücken” = to bend down, stoop (reflexive). damit = so that (purpose), sending its verb to the end. hineinkommen (to get in) is separable: “hinein… kam.”

sentences.t: „Wo sind die Diener?“ sprach die Königstochter.
sentences.l: “Where are the servants?” spoke the king’s daughter.
sentences.i: “Where are the servants?” said the king’s daughter.

sentences.t: „Was Diener!“ antwortete der Bettelmann, „du mußt selber thun was du willst gethan haben.
sentences.l: “What servants!” answered the beggar-man, “you must yourself do what you want done.
sentences.i: “What servants!” answered the beggar-man; “you must do yourself whatever you want done.
sentences.n: A scornful “Was Diener!” = “Servants, indeed!” “was du willst gethan haben” = what you want (to have) done. thun/gethan are the 1857 spellings of tun/getan.

sentences.t: Mach nur gleich Feuer an und stell Wasser auf, daß du mir mein Essen kochst; ich bin ganz müde.“
sentences.l: Just make a fire at once and put water on, so that you cook me my meal; I am quite tired.”
sentences.i: Just light a fire straight away and put water on to boil, so you can cook my meal; I am quite worn out.”
sentences.n: Two separable verbs give orders: anmachen (‘mach… an’, light) and aufstellen (‘stell… auf’, put on). kochst is 2nd singular of kochen. The pampered princess is set to menial work.

sentences.t: Die Königstochter verstand aber nichts vom Feueranmachen und Kochen, und der Bettelmann mußte selber mit Hand anlegen, daß es noch so leidlich gieng.
sentences.l: But the king’s daughter understood nothing of fire-making and cooking, and the beggar-man had to lend a hand himself, so that it went even tolerably well.
sentences.i: But the king’s daughter knew nothing of laying fires or cooking, and the beggar-man had to lend a hand himself to make it go even passably.
sentences.n: “verstehen von” = to know about, be versed in. “mit Hand anlegen” = to lend a hand (anlegen separable). “so leidlich” = tolerably, passably.

sentences.t: Als sie die schmale Kost verzehrt hatten, legten sie sich zu Bett: aber am Morgen trieb er sie schon ganz früh heraus, weil sie das Haus besorgen sollte.
sentences.l: When they had consumed the meagre fare, they laid themselves to bed: but in the morning he drove her out quite early already, because she was to look after the house.
sentences.i: When they had eaten the meagre fare, they went to bed; but in the morning he drove her out very early, since she was to keep the house.
sentences.n: “schmale Kost” = meagre fare, thin rations. “sich zu Bett legen” = to go to bed. heraustreiben (to drive out) is separable: “trieb… heraus.” besorgen = to see to, look after.

sentences.t: Ein paar Tage lebten sie auf diese Art schlecht und recht, und zehrten ihren Vorrath auf.
sentences.l: A couple of days they lived in this manner middling well, and used up their store.
sentences.i: For a couple of days they lived after this fashion, so-so, and ate through their provisions.
sentences.n: “auf diese Art” = in this way. “schlecht und recht” = after a fashion, passably (a set phrase). aufzehren (to use up, consume) is separable: “zehrten… auf”; der Vorrath (1857 for Vorrat) = store, provisions.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Mann „Frau, so gehts nicht länger, daß wir hier zehren und nichts verdienen.
sentences.l: Then the man spoke “wife, so it goes no longer, that we here consume and earn nothing.
sentences.i: Then the man said, “Wife, it can’t go on like this, our living here and earning nothing.
sentences.n: “so gehts nicht länger” = it can’t go on like this (gehts = geht es). zehren = to live off one’s store, consume provisions.

sentences.t: Du sollst Körbe flechten.“
sentences.l: You shall plait baskets.”
sentences.i: You shall weave baskets.”
sentences.n: “Körbe flechten” = to plait/weave baskets (Korb, pl Körbe). sollen here = ‘are to, shall,’ a command.

sentences.t: Er gieng aus, schnitt Weiden, und brachte sie heim: da fieng sie an zu flechten, aber die harten Weiden stachen ihr die zarten Hände wund.
sentences.l: He went out, cut willow-branches, and brought them home: then she began to plait, but the hard willows pricked her tender hands sore.
sentences.i: He went out, cut willows, and brought them home; then she set to plaiting, but the hard willow-rods pricked her tender hands raw.
sentences.n: ausgehen (to go out) separable: “gieng aus” (gieng is 1857 for ging). anfangen (to begin) separable: “fieng… an” (fieng for fing). “wund stechen” = to prick sore/raw; stachen is past of stechen.

sentences.t: „Ich sehe das geht nicht,“ sprach der Mann, „spinn lieber, vielleicht kannst du das besser.“
sentences.l: “I see this does not work,” spoke the man, “spin rather, perhaps you can do that better.”
sentences.i: “I see this won’t do,” said the man; “rather spin — perhaps you can manage that better.”
sentences.n: “das geht nicht” = that won’t do. spinn is the imperative of spinnen (to spin). lieber = rather, sooner.

sentences.t: Sie setzte sich hin, und versuchte zu spinnen, aber der harte Faden schnitt ihr bald in die weichen Finger, daß das Blut daran herunter lief.
sentences.l: She sat herself down, and tried to spin, but the hard thread soon cut her in the soft fingers, so that the blood ran down from it.
sentences.i: She sat down and tried to spin, but the hard thread soon cut into her soft fingers, so that the blood ran down them.
sentences.n: “sich hinsetzen” = to sit down (hinsetzen separable). der Faden = thread. “schnitt ihr… in die Finger” — dative ihr for the person whose fingers are cut. herunterlaufen (to run down) separable.

sentences.t: „Siehst du,“ sprach der Mann, „du taugst zu keiner Arbeit, mit dir bin ich schlimm angekommen.
sentences.l: “You see,” spoke the man, “you are fit for no work, with you I have come off badly.
sentences.i: “You see,” said the man, “you’re fit for no work; I’ve done badly with you.
sentences.n: “zu keiner Arbeit taugen” = to be fit for no work (taugen = to be good for). “schlimm angekommen sein” = to have come off badly, be in a fix (ankommen).

sentences.t: Nun will ichs versuchen, und einen Handel mit Töpfen und irdenem Geschirr anfangen: du sollst dich auf den Markt setzen, und die Waare feil halten.“
sentences.l: Now I will try it, and start a trade with pots and earthen crockery: you shall set yourself at the market, and offer the wares for sale.”
sentences.i: Now I’ll try starting a trade in pots and earthenware: you shall sit at the market and offer the wares for sale.”
sentences.n: einen Handel anfangen = to start a trade. “irdenes Geschirr” = earthenware, earthen crockery. “feil halten” = to keep on offer, offer for sale (feil = for sale). die Waare (1857 for Ware) = wares.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ dachte sie, „wenn auf den Markt Leute aus meines Vaters Reich kommen, und sehen mich da sitzen und feil halten, wie werden sie mich verspotten!“
sentences.l: “Ah,” thought she, “when to the market people come from my father’s realm, and see me sitting there and offering for sale, how will they mock me!”
sentences.i: “Ah,” she thought, “when folk from my father’s kingdom come to the market and see me sitting there hawking wares, how they will mock me!”
sentences.n: “meines Vaters Reich” = my father’s realm (genitive meines). verspotten = to mock, deride — the very thing she once did to her suitors, now feared against herself.

sentences.t: Aber es half nichts, sie mußte sich fügen, wenn sie nicht Hungers sterben wollten.
sentences.l: But it helped nothing, she had to comply, if they did not want to die of hunger.
sentences.i: But it was no use; she had to submit, unless they wanted to starve.
sentences.n: “es half nichts” = it was no use. “sich fügen” = to give in, comply. “Hungers sterben” = to die of hunger (Hungers is an old genitive). The plural wollten shifts to ‘they’ — both of them.

sentences.t: Das erstemal gings gut, denn die Leute kauften der Frau, weil sie schön war, gern ihre Waare ab, und bezahlten was sie forderte: ja, viele gaben ihr das Geld, und ließen ihr die Töpfe noch dazu.
sentences.l: The first time it went well, for the people bought the woman’s wares gladly, because she was beautiful, and paid what she demanded: yes, many gave her the money, and left her the pots into the bargain.
sentences.i: The first time it went well, for the people gladly bought the woman’s wares because she was beautiful, and paid whatever she asked — indeed, many gave her the money and left her the pots as well.
sentences.n: “das erstemal” = the first time. “gings gut” = it went well (gings = ging es). abkaufen = to buy from someone (kauften… ab). forderte = demanded. “noch dazu” = into the bargain, on top of that.

sentences.t: Nun lebten sie von dem erworbenen so lang es dauerte, da handelte der Mann wieder eine Menge neues Geschirr ein.
sentences.l: Now they lived off the earned so long as it lasted, then the man bought in again a great quantity of new crockery.
sentences.i: They lived off what she had taken in as long as it lasted, and then the man bought in a fresh lot of crockery again.
sentences.n: “von dem erworbenen” = off what had been earned (erworben, past participle of erwerben used as a noun). einhandeln (to buy in) separable: “handelte… ein.” “eine Menge” = a lot, a quantity.

sentences.t: Sie setzte sich damit an eine Ecke des Marktes, und stellte es um sich her, und hielt feil.
sentences.l: She sat herself down with it at a corner of the market, and set it around herself, and held it for sale.
sentences.i: She settled herself with it at a corner of the market, set it out around her, and offered it for sale.
sentences.n: “an eine Ecke des Marktes” = at a corner of the market (Marktes, genitive of Markt). “um sich her” = round about her. “feil halten” again = to offer for sale.

sentences.t: Da kam plötzlich ein trunkener Husar daher gejagt, und ritt gerade zu in die Töpfe hinein, daß alles in tausend Scherben zersprang.
sentences.l: There came suddenly a drunken hussar galloping up, and rode straight into the pots, so that everything burst into a thousand shards.
sentences.i: Suddenly a drunken hussar came galloping up and rode straight into the pots, so that everything shattered into a thousand shards.
sentences.n: ein Husar = a hussar, a light-cavalry soldier. “daher gejagt kommen” = to come racing/galloping up (jagen). zerspringen = to burst apart, shatter (zersprang). The disaster is, of course, arranged.

sentences.t: Sie fieng an zu weinen und wußte vor Angst nicht was sie anfangen sollte.
sentences.l: She began to weep and knew for fear not what she should set about.
sentences.i: She began to weep and, for fear, did not know what to do.
sentences.n: anfangen (to begin) separable: “fieng an.” “vor Angst” = for/out of fear. “was sie anfangen sollte” = what she should do/undertake (anfangen also = to set about).

sentences.t: „Ach, wie wird mirs ergehen!“ rief sie, „was wird mein Mann dazu sagen!“
sentences.l: “Ah, how will it go with me!” cried she, “what will my husband say to it!”
sentences.i: “Ah, what will become of me!” she cried; “what will my husband say to this!”
sentences.n: “wie wird mirs ergehen” = how will I fare, what will become of me (mirs = mir es; ergehen, impersonal with dative).

sentences.t: Sie lief heim und erzählte ihm das Unglück.
sentences.l: She ran home and told him the misfortune.
sentences.i: She ran home and told him of the mishap.

sentences.t: „Wer setzt sich auch an die Ecke des Marktes mit irdenem Geschirr!“ sprach der Mann, „laß nur das Weinen, ich sehe wohl du bist zu keiner ordentlichen Arbeit zu gebrauchen.
sentences.l: “Who, after all, sits down at the corner of the market with earthen crockery!” spoke the man, “stop the weeping now, I see well you are to be used for no proper work.
sentences.i: “Who on earth sits down at a market corner with earthenware!” said the man. “Now stop your weeping — I see plainly you’re fit for no proper work.
sentences.n: “wer setzt sich auch…” — auch here adds an exasperated “who on earth…?” “laß… das Weinen” = leave off the weeping. “zu keiner Arbeit zu gebrauchen” = fit for no work (gebrauchen = to use).

sentences.t: Da bin ich in unseres Königs Schloß gewesen und habe gefragt ob sie nicht eine Küchenmagd brauchen könnten, und sie haben mir versprochen sie wollten dich dazu nehmen; dafür bekommst du freies Essen.“
sentences.l: There have I been in our king’s castle and have asked whether they could not use a kitchen-maid, and they have promised me they would take you for it; for that you get free food.”
sentences.i: I’ve been to our king’s castle and asked whether they could use a kitchen-maid, and they promised me they would take you on; for that you’ll get your food free.”
sentences.n: “unseres Königs Schloß” = our king’s castle (genitive). eine Küchenmagd = a kitchen-maid. könnten and wollten are subjunctive of reported speech (‘could,’ ‘would’). “freies Essen” = free board. Note the irony: the castle is her own father’s.

sentences.t: Nun ward die Königstochter eine Küchenmagd, mußte dem Koch zur Hand gehen und die sauerste Arbeit thun.
sentences.l: Now the king’s daughter became a kitchen-maid, had to go to the cook’s hand and do the sourest work.
sentences.i: Now the king’s daughter became a kitchen-maid; she had to assist the cook and do the hardest work.
sentences.n: “jemandem zur Hand gehen” = to lend someone a hand, assist. “die sauerste Arbeit” = the hardest, most disagreeable work (sauer, superlative sauerste, ‘sour’ → ‘hard, galling’). thun = 1857 for tun.

sentences.t: Sie machte sich in beiden Taschen ein Töpfchen fest, darin brachte sie nach Haus was ihr von dem übrig gebliebenen zu Theil ward, und davon nährten sie sich.
sentences.l: She fastened a little pot for herself in both pockets, in it she brought home what fell to her share of the leftovers, and from that they nourished themselves.
sentences.i: She fastened a little pot into each of her pockets, and in it she carried home whatever came to her of the leftovers, and on that they fed themselves.
sentences.n: ein Töpfchen = a little pot (diminutive of Topf). “von dem übrig gebliebenen” = of the leftovers (übrig geblieben, ‘remained over’). “zu Theil werden” = to fall to one’s share (Theil = 1857 for Teil). nähren = to nourish.

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, daß die Hochzeit des ältesten Königssohnes sollte gefeiert werden, da gieng die arme Frau hinauf, stellte sich vor die Saalthüre und wollte zusehen.
sentences.l: It came to pass that the wedding of the eldest king’s-son was to be celebrated, then the poor woman went up, placed herself before the hall-door and wanted to look on.
sentences.i: It came to pass that the wedding of the eldest prince was to be celebrated; so the poor woman went up, took her place at the hall door, and wanted to watch.
sentences.n: “es trug sich zu” = it came to pass (sich zutragen). “des ältesten Königssohnes” = the eldest king’s son (genitive). die Saalthüre = the hall door (Saal + Thüre). zusehen = to look on, watch.

sentences.t: Als nun die Lichter angezündet waren, und immer einer schöner als der andere hereintrat, und alles voll Pracht und Herrlichkeit war, da dachte sie mit betrübtem Herzen an ihr Schicksal, und verwünschte ihren Stolz und Übermuth, der sie erniedrigt und in so große Armuth gestürzt hatte.
sentences.l: When now the lights were kindled, and ever one entered more beautiful than the other, and everything was full of pomp and splendour, then she thought with grieved heart of her fate, and cursed her pride and haughtiness, which had abased her and cast her into so great poverty.
sentences.i: When the lights were kindled, and one guest entered ever more splendid than the last, and all was full of pomp and magnificence, she thought with a grieving heart of her lot, and cursed the pride and arrogance that had brought her low and plunged her into such great poverty.
sentences.n: “immer einer schöner als der andere” = each one finer than the last. hereintreten (to enter) separable: “hereintrat.” “mit betrübtem Herzen” = with a grieved heart. Übermuth (1857 for Übermut) = arrogance, the very fault the tale punishes. erniedrigen = to humble; stürzen = to cast, plunge.

sentences.t: Von den köstlichen Speisen, die da ein und ausgetragen wurden, und von welchen der Geruch zu ihr aufstieg, warfen ihr Diener manchmal ein paar Brocken zu, die that sie in ihr Töpfchen, und wollte es heim tragen.
sentences.l: Of the delicious dishes, which were carried in and out there, and of which the smell rose up to her, servants sometimes threw her a couple of scraps, those she put into her little pot, and wanted to carry it home.
sentences.i: Of the delicious dishes that were carried in and out, and whose smell rose up to her, the servants sometimes tossed her a few scraps; these she put into her little pot, meaning to carry it home.
sentences.n: “ein und ausgetragen” = carried in and out (aus/eintragen). “von welchen der Geruch” = of which the smell (welchen, relative). zuwerfen (to throw to) separable: “warfen… zu.” die that = ‘those she put’ (fronted for emphasis; that = 1857 for tat). This little pot sets up the humiliation to come.

sentences.t: Auf einmal trat der Königssohn herein, war in Sammt und Seide gekleidet und hatte goldene Ketten um den Hals.
sentences.l: All at once the king’s son stepped in, was clad in velvet and silk and had golden chains about the neck.
sentences.i: All at once the prince stepped in, clad in velvet and silk, with golden chains about his neck.
sentences.n: “auf einmal” = all at once. hereintreten again: “trat… herein.” “in Sammt und Seide gekleidet” = clothed in velvet and silk (Sammt = 1857 for Samt).

sentences.t: Und als er die schöne Frau in der Thüre stehen sah, ergriff er sie bei der Hand, und wollte mit ihr tanzen, aber sie weigerte sich und erschrack, denn sie sah daß es der König Drosselbart war, der um sie gefreit und den sie mit Spott abgewiesen hatte.
sentences.l: And when he saw the beautiful woman standing in the doorway, he seized her by the hand, and wanted to dance with her, but she resisted herself and took fright, for she saw that it was King Thrushbeard, who had courted her and whom she had rejected with mockery.
sentences.i: And when he saw the beautiful woman standing in the doorway, he seized her by the hand and wanted to dance with her; but she resisted and was terrified, for she saw that it was King Thrushbeard, who had courted her and whom she had rejected with scorn.
sentences.n: ergreifen = to seize (ergriff). “sich weigern” = to refuse, resist. erschrack = took fright (archaic). “um sie gefreit” = had wooed her (freien, ‘to woo, court’). abweisen (to reject) again: the wheel comes round.

sentences.t: Ihr Sträuben half nichts, er zog sie in den Saal: da zerriß das Band, an welchem die Taschen hiengen, und die Töpfe fielen heraus, daß die Suppe floß und die Brocken umher sprangen.
sentences.l: Her resisting helped nothing, he drew her into the hall: then tore the band, on which the pockets hung, and the pots fell out, so that the soup flowed and the scraps sprang about.
sentences.i: Her struggling was no use; he drew her into the hall — whereupon the band the pockets hung by tore, and the pots fell out, so that the soup ran and the scraps scattered about.
sentences.n: “Ihr Sträuben” = her struggling (from sich sträuben, to resist). zerreißen = to tear apart (zerriß). “an welchem die Taschen hiengen” = on which the pockets hung (hiengen = 1857 for hingen). Her hidden shame spills out in front of all.

sentences.t: Und wie das die Leute sahen, entstand ein allgemeines Gelächter und Spotten, und sie war so beschämt, daß sie sich lieber tausend Klafter unter die Erde gewünscht hätte.
sentences.l: And as the people saw that, there arose a general laughter and mocking, and she was so ashamed, that she would rather have wished herself a thousand fathoms under the earth.
sentences.i: And when the people saw it, a general laughter and jeering broke out, and she was so ashamed that she would sooner have wished herself a thousand fathoms under the earth.
sentences.n: entstehen = to arise, break out (entstand). das Gelächter = laughter; Spotten = mocking (the tale’s recurring note, now turned on her). “tausend Klafter” = a thousand fathoms (Klafter, an old depth-measure). “gewünscht hätte” = would have wished (past subjunctive).

sentences.t: Sie sprang zur Thüre hinaus und wollte entfliehen, aber auf der Treppe holte sie ein Mann ein, und brachte sie zurück: und wie sie ihn ansah, war es wieder der König Drosselbart.
sentences.l: She sprang out of the door and wanted to flee, but on the stairs a man caught her up, and brought her back: and as she looked at him, it was again King Thrushbeard.
sentences.i: She sprang out of the door and tried to flee, but on the stairs a man overtook her and brought her back; and when she looked at him, it was King Thrushbeard once more.
sentences.n: “zur Thüre hinaus” = out through the door. einholen (to catch up, overtake) separable: “holte… ein.” ansehen (to look at) separable: “ansah.” The disguises begin to fall away.

sentences.t: Er sprach ihr freundlich zu, „fürchte dich nicht, ich und der Spielmann, der mit dir in dem elenden Häuschen gewohnt hat, sind eins: dir zu Liebe habe ich mich so verstellt, und der Husar, der dir die Töpfe entzwei geritten hat, bin ich auch gewesen.
sentences.l: He spoke to her kindly, “fear yourself not, I and the minstrel, who dwelt with you in the wretched little house, are one: for love of you I disguised myself so, and the hussar, who rode your pots in two, I have been also.
sentences.i: He spoke to her kindly: “Do not be afraid — I and the minstrel who lived with you in the wretched little house are one and the same. For love of you I disguised myself so; and the hussar who broke your pots to pieces was I as well.
sentences.n: zusprechen (to speak to) separable: “sprach… zu.” “fürchte dich nicht” = don’t be afraid (sich fürchten). “dir zu Liebe” = for love of you, for your sake. “sich verstellen” = to disguise oneself. entzwei reiten = to ride to pieces (entzwei = in two). The whole ordeal is revealed as staged.

sentences.t: Das alles ist geschehen, um deinen stolzen Sinn zu beugen, und dich für deinen Hochmuth zu strafen, womit du mich verspottet hast.“
sentences.l: All that has happened, in order to bend your proud spirit, and to punish you for your arrogance, wherewith you mocked me.”
sentences.i: All this has happened to bend your proud spirit and to punish you for the arrogance with which you mocked me.”
sentences.n: “um… zu” + infinitive twice = in order to. beugen = to bend, humble; “deinen stolzen Sinn” = your proud spirit. der Hochmuth (1857 for Hochmut) = arrogance, overweening pride. womit = with which, wherewith.

sentences.t: Da weinte sie bitterlich und sagte „ich habe großes Unrecht gehabt und bin nicht werth deine Frau zu sein.“
sentences.l: Then wept she bitterly and said “I have had great wrong and am not worth to be your wife.”
sentences.i: Then she wept bitterly and said, “I have done a great wrong and am not worthy to be your wife.”
sentences.n: “Unrecht haben” = to be in the wrong (Unrecht = wrong, injustice). werth (1857 for wert) = worthy. Her pride finally breaks.

sentences.t: Er aber sprach „tröste dich, die bösen Tage sind vorüber, jetzt wollen wir unsere Hochzeit feiern.“
sentences.l: But he spoke “comfort yourself, the evil days are over, now we will celebrate our wedding.”
sentences.i: But he said, “Take comfort — the evil days are past; now we will celebrate our wedding.”
sentences.n: “tröste dich” = take comfort (sich trösten). “die bösen Tage sind vorüber” = the bad days are over. feiern = to celebrate.

sentences.t: Da kamen die Kammerfrauen und thaten ihr die prächtigsten Kleider an, und ihr Vater kam und der ganze Hof, und wünschten ihr Glück zu ihrer Vermählung mit dem König Drosselbart, und die rechte Freude fieng jetzt erst an.
sentences.l: Then came the chamber-women and put on her the most magnificent clothes, and her father came and the whole court, and wished her joy at her marriage with King Thrushbeard, and the true joy began now for the first time.
sentences.i: Then the chambermaids came and dressed her in the most magnificent clothes, and her father came, and the whole court, and wished her joy at her marriage to King Thrushbeard; and the real rejoicing now began at last.
sentences.n: die Kammerfrauen = the chambermaids, ladies of the wardrobe. antun (to put on) separable: “thaten… an” (thaten = 1857 for taten). die Vermählung = the marriage, wedding. “jetzt erst” = only now, at last.

sentences.t: Ich wollte, du und ich, wir wären auch dabei gewesen.
sentences.l: I wished, you and I, we had been there too.
sentences.i: I could wish that you and I had been there as well.
sentences.n: The teller’s closing wink, straight to the listener. “ich wollte” = I would wish. wären is past subjunctive (‘had been’). A traditional close, like the ‘and they lived happily ever after’ aside.
